turns.response_text:
Nike was co-founded by **Phil Knight** and **Bill Bowerman**.

Knight is the central figure in the Nike origin story — covered extensively in episode 10 on *Shoe Dog*, his memoir. He started the venture in 1964 (originally as Blue Ribbon Sports) after a "crazy idea" epiphany during a morning run in 1962, selling Japanese running shoes out of the trunk of his car [ep-0010].

Bowerman was Knight's track coach at the University of Oregon and his eventual business partner. He was the obsessive engineer of the duo — maniacally focused on reducing shoe weight because he believed "lightness directly translated to less burden, which meant more energy, which meant more speed and speed equaled winning" [ep-0010]. His first-principles thinking about athletics (he pioneered the "hard-easy" training method when it was scorned by other coaches) and his hands-on shoe tinkering — famously creating the waffle sole in his own kitchen — became foundational to Nike's culture and competitive edge [ep-0153].

So it's a classic two-founder dynamic: Knight brought the entrepreneurial drive and vision, Bowerman brought the coach's engineering mindset and relentless focus on marginal gains.

turns.action: send 'What book did he rely on?'
turns.response_text:
The vault doesn’t actually point to a specific book Phil Knight relied on to guide him. What it *does* surface — across multiple episodes — is that the intellectual scaffolding for Nike wasn't a book he read, but a **paper he wrote himself** at Stanford Business School in 1962.

The paper was titled: *"Can Japanese Sports Shoes Do to German Sports Shoes What Japanese Cameras Did to German Cameras?"* [ep-0022, ep-0153, ep-0161]. That analogy — Japanese manufacturers had already displaced German dominance in cameras and electronics, so why not in athletic shoes? — became the thesis on which he built the entire venture. As David Senra puts it in the Bill Bowerman episode: "That was the thesis on which he built Nike on, which wound up being correct" [ep-0153].

This is actually a neat pattern in itself. Knight didn't find his roadmap in someone else's book; he looked at the historical precedent playing out in a parallel industry and drew a straight line. His Stanford classmates were bored by the paper [ep-0022], but the idea was essentially the entire business plan.

If you're asking because you remember him mentioning a specific title in *Shoe Dog* itself, that detail doesn't appear in the vault's coverage of the episode — which is possible given that Senra explicitly says his goal isn't to summarize the whole book, just to pull out the parts that surprised him [ep-0010]. So the honest answer is: the vault is thin on any single book Knight relied on, and his proven "guide" was his own cross-industry analogy.
